# Changelog

## [Unreleased]

### Added

- **Learning-to-rank feature selection (LTR-local, core MRMR untouched).** The LTR ranker suite now supports opt-in MRMR feature selection via `LearningToRankConfig.feature_selection=True`. A new, SEPARATE module `training/ranking/_ranker_fs.py` orchestrates a standard `MRMR.fit` on the TRAIN split's (features, graded relevance) and selects the raw columns every ranker then trains on — it does NOT modify the highly-optimised core MRMR/MI/binning procedures. Knobs on `LearningToRankConfig`: `feature_selection` (default OFF), `fs_mrmr_kwargs` (forwarded to the selector's `MRMR(...)`), `fs_group_aware_mi` (use a self-contained per-query binned MI relevance pre-filter — a ranking-appropriate, query-respecting relevance signal — instead of pointwise MI, again without touching the core group-naive MI). Selection is stamped into `metadata["selected_features"]`. **Fix (same change):** the ranker suite now drops the *resolved* relevance column from the feature matrix — FTEs that declare the target via typed lists (e.g. `learning_to_rank_targets=[...]`, no `target_column` attr) previously leaked the relevance column into X, letting rankers memorise it (target leak). Tests: `tests/training/ranking/test_ltr_feature_selection.py` (unit selector + group-aware MI biz_value + e2e suite FS + relevance-leak regression).

- **Cross-instance per-column code cache for unsupervised discretization.** `categorize_dataset`'s unsupervised numeric path (`nbins_strategy=None`) now memoises per-column ordinal codes in a process-wide LRU cache (`_discretize_2d_array_col_cached`), keyed on a content hash of (column values, n_bins, method, min_ncats, dtype). Across a suite training MANY targets on the SAME feature frame, each feature column recurs byte-identically while only the injected target column is new, so the 2nd..Nth target reuses the feature codes and rebins only the target — bit-identical by construction. Deliberately NOT used on the SUPERVISED `nbins_strategy` path (mdlp/optimal_joint/…), where bins depend on `y` and a feature's codes legitimately differ per target (so cross-target sharing would be wrong); under the MRMR default (`nbins_strategy="mdlp"`) the cache is a correct no-op. Byte-budgeted (`MLFRAME_DISCRETIZE_COL_CACHE_MAX_BYTES`, default 512 MB) so a 100 GB-frame fit cannot pin unbounded RAM; disable via `MLFRAME_DISCRETIZE_COL_CACHE=0`; `clear_numeric_code_cache()` drains it. Audit context: MRMR weight schemas already share a single FS fit (no separate instances needed); feature↔target relevance is genuinely per-target. Tests: `tests/feature_selection/test_discretize_col_cache.py`.
- **`SimpleFeaturesAndTargetsExtractor.learning_to_rank_targets`.** The production extractor can now declare a graded-relevance LEARNING_TO_RANK target directly (previously only regression/classification); pairs with `group_field` for per-query grouping (warns when absent). Tests: `tests/training/extractors/test_extractors.py`.
- **Generic estimator-instance support in the suite.** `mlframe_models` now accepts sklearn-compatible estimator INSTANCES and `(name, estimator)` tuples alongside the built-in string tags (`cb`/`lgb`/`xgb`/`hgb`/`mlp`/`ngb`/`linear`). `configure_training_params` builds a minimal `dict(model=est)` params entry for each non-string entry; `get_strategy` already dispatches instances MRO-based (unknown → `LinearModelStrategy`); the per-target training loop (`_phase_train_one_target_body`) now keys `models_params` / `strategy_by_model` by the entry object / `id()` and derives a string label for `model_category` / file names / logging, so an injected estimator is actually trained, predicted, and saved/loaded. String-tag runs are byte-identical. Tests: `tests/training/test_custom_estimator_instance_suite.py`.
- **Distribution-driven composite estimator (E3) now TRAINS, not just advises.** When the target-distribution analyzer flags a heavy-tail / skew / multi-modal regression target and `behavior_config.distribution_driven_estimator=True` (default OFF), `composite._estimator_dispatch.maybe_inject_distribution_driven_estimator` appends the recommended estimator (heavy-tail/skew → `TailCompositeEstimator`, multi-modal → `CompositeDistributionEstimator`) to `mlframe_models` with an auto-picked `base_column` (max |corr| to y) + a light GBM base, and extends `ctx.strategy_by_model` / `ctx.sorted_mlframe_models` / `ctx.mlframe_models` so the per-target loop fits it alongside the requested models; the decision is stamped into `metadata["distribution_driven_estimator"]`. This lands on the generic estimator-instance path above, resolving both prior blockers (base_column auto-selected; instance training supported). `recommend_composite_estimator` / `instantiate_recommended_estimator` stay available; finalize still stamps the advisory `metadata["composite_estimator_recommendation"]`. biz_value: on a Student-t(df=2.5) heavy-tail target the GPD-extrapolated 0.999 upper-tail quantile tracks nominal coverage ~14× closer than a Gaussian-tail assumption. Tests: `tests/training/test_estimator_dispatch.py`, `tests/training/test_distribution_driven_estimator_suite.py`, `tests/training/test_biz_val_training_distribution_driven.py`.

- **`TrainingSplitConfig.time_column` + `cv_strategy` (E2) and a pre-discovery base-target leakage guard (E1).** E1: `CompositeTargetDiscovery.fit` now runs `detect_base_target_leakage` over the resolved base candidates (`config.detect_base_leakage`, default ON), dropping same-time near-identity re-encodings of `y` (target-encoding / rolling-target bases the `forbidden_base` regex misses) — gated on a supplied `time_ordering` so the lag-probe spares a genuine `lag(y)` base and autocorrelation is never mistaken for leakage (no-op on cross-sectional data). E2-surface: `time_column` (None) + `cv_strategy` ("random"/"timeseries"/"purged") declare temporal-split intent; consumed today by the conformal structure-inference (so split-conformal marginal-coverage validity is reported honestly on temporal splits) with a `cv_strategy`×`val_placement='backward'` conflict validator; routing the main split through the purged forward-walk lands in a follow-up. Tests: `test_base_leakage_guard.py`, `test_split_config_time_fields.py`.

- **Uncertainty / robustness cores (Workstream B, opt-in).** `training/_mc_dropout.py` — `mc_dropout_predict` keeps only dropout modules stochastic at inference (BatchNorm/LayerNorm stay in eval), returns predictive mean + (approximate, uncalibrated) spread, restores module mode; `predictive_entropy` for the classification uncertainty summary. `training/_tta.py` — `tta_predict` averages predictions over per-feature-jittered input copies (mean / median; probability-mean stays on the simplex) + `tta_predict_spread`. `training/_noise_ensemble.py` — `NoiseAugmentedEnsemble` trains k clones on jittered inputs and averages, scoped to high-variance NON-TREE learners (measured ~34% RMSE cut on 1-NN; a measured LOSS on low-variance OLS is pinned too, REJECTED!=DELETED). All model-agnostic, tested, default-off, and exposed as **public predict-time utilities** on `mlframe.training` (`tta_predict`, `tta_predict_spread`, `evaluate_tta_quality`, `mc_dropout_predict`, `predictive_entropy`, `NoiseAugmentedEnsemble`). **In-suite TTA uncertainty evaluation on the honest test split is now wired**: with `behavior_config.uncertainty_eval=True`, after each regression model trains, the suite evaluates TTA quality on the live model-ready transformed test frame and stamps `metadata["uncertainty_eval"][target/model] = {test: {rmse_point, rmse_tta, tta_rmse_gain, spread_error_corr, mean_spread}}` (numeric-feature regression models; default OFF, best-effort, no frames carried on saved models). `evaluate_tta_quality(predict_fn, X, y)` is also callable standalone on any held-out (X, y). Tests: `test_mc_dropout.py`, `test_tta_predict.py`, `test_noise_ensemble.py`, `test_uncertainty_public_api.py`, `test_uncertainty_eval_holdout.py` (incl. e2e suite run).

- **Curated feature-engineering allowlist (Workstream C): `feature_engineering.curated_fe.curated_fe_pipelines(...)`.** Exposes the 5 highest-evidence, leak-safe shortlist transformers (nn_oof_target_mean, multi_aux_ensemble, baseline_disagreement, trust_score_oof, y_quintile_baseline_knn) from the otherwise-dark 100+ `transformer/` library, each wrapped via `ShortlistTransformerAdapter` into a sklearn pipeline ready to drop into `FeatureSelectionConfig.custom_pre_pipelines` (already consumed by the suite — no pipeline-phase wiring). Opt-in: nothing enabled implicitly; call the factory (`task`, `names`, `seed`, `passthrough`) and pass the result. Tests: `tests/feature_selection/test_curated_fe_allowlist.py`.

- **D-flip campaign verdict: NO FS default flipped (levers stay opt-in).** The committed bench (`feature_selection/_benchmarks/bench_fs_levers_dflip.py`, 5 seeds × 3 scenarios, JSON in `tests/perf/results/dflip_fs_levers.json`) measured the surfaced levers on honest-holdout R²: `mi_normalization=su` HURTS the synergy scenario (−0.04) and is neutral elsewhere; `redundancy_aggregator=jmim` wins synergy (+0.197) but only 1/3 scenarios (never a majority); su+jmim COMBINED HURTS (−0.018, a real negative interaction). Per the accuracy-first rule (flip only on a combined-config majority win) none qualify — defaults unchanged; the levers remain valuable opt-ins via the D-surface fields. (REJECTED≠DELETED: bench committed for re-validation.)

- **Feature-selection levers surfaced as first-class `FeatureSelectionConfig` fields (D-surface).** Promotes undocumented `mrmr_kwargs`/`rfecv_kwargs` knobs to named, discoverable fields: RFECV `rfecv_must_include`/`rfecv_must_exclude`/`rfecv_feature_groups`/`rfecv_n_features_selection_rule`/`rfecv_enable_stability_selection`/`rfecv_enable_permutation_importance`/`rfecv_prescreen`/`rfecv_swap_top_k`; MRMR `mrmr_mi_normalization`/`mrmr_redundancy_aggregator`/`mrmr_cpt_test`/`mrmr_uaed_auto_size`/`mrmr_pid_synergy_bonus`/`mrmr_mi_correction`. A model-validator folds set levers into the kwargs dicts and RAISES if a key is ALSO passed explicitly (silent-override guard). All default to the unset sentinel, so a config that sets no lever is byte-identical to today — flipping any default ON is a separate bench-gated step (D-flip). Tests: `tests/training/test_fs_levers_surface.py`.

- **Conformal prediction intervals + achieved coverage, and classification prediction sets, in `train_mlframe_models_suite` finalize.** The suite previously shipped only point predictions; it now stamps distribution-free uncertainty into `metadata["conformal"]` per per-target model. Regression: split-conformal intervals (absolute / locally-adaptive normalized score) from the leakage-free `calib_size` slice — the trainer now stamps regression calib-slice point predictions (`entry.calib_preds`) as the residual source — falling back to CV+/Jackknife+ from OOF residuals (honestly labelled `>=1-2alpha` vs split-conformal's `>=1-alpha`); per-alpha achieved coverage / mean width / Winkler on the honest test split. Classification: split-conformal prediction SETS (LAC default, APS optional via `classification_mode`) from the already-stamped calib probs, with marginal coverage + mean set size. A `SplitStructure` tag inferred from the split config (iid / temporal / grouped / temporal_grouped / stratified) drives a `split_conformal_valid_for_structure` flag so non-iid splits (temporal/grouped) do not silently report invalid marginal coverage. New modules `training/_conformal_finalize.py` (model-agnostic core, reuses `composite.conformal.conformal_quantile`, the sigma-model helpers, `composite.conformal_classification.conformal_set_threshold`, and `metrics.quantile` coverage metrics), `training/_conformal_split.py` (structure-aware calib/conformal slice carving), finalize hook `_conformal_on_calib_slice`. Additive + best-effort: reads only already-stamped arrays, never mutates a model, clean no-op when no calib/OOF data. New `TrainingSplitConfig.conformal_size` second holdout (default None) reserved for scoring the recalibrated predictor; the classification calib path is unchanged. biz_value: normalized score cuts the heteroscedastic conditional-coverage gap ~0.3 -> <0.15 while staying ~nominal marginally; LAC/APS sets hit >=1-alpha marginal coverage on a 3-class synthetic. Tests: 5 new files (conformal core, classification sets, carving, finalize hook, e2e suite run).

- **Regression point recalibration (opt-in): a monotone `g(yhat)~=E[y|yhat]`** (`training/_regression_calibration.py`: `PointRecalibrator` isotonic/linear, `RecalibratedRegressor` picklable wrapper, `recalibration_rmse_gain` + `cv2_recalibration_gain` honest 2-fold-within-calib gate). The classification analog (Platt/isotonic) is standard; this is its underused regression counterpart, correcting shrinkage (ridge / early-stopped GBM / tree averaging compress the tails) without changing ranking. Finalize hook `_recalibrate_regression_on_calib_slice` fits `g` on the calib slice and applies it ONLY when the 2-fold gain beats `min_gain`, wrapping the model + re-stamping cached preds so the conformal pass scores the shipped recalibrated predictor. Default OFF (gate + `regression_calibration_config.point` / `MLFRAME_REGRESSION_RECALIBRATION` env). biz_value: isotonic recovers a shrunk t(3) predictor for RMSE gain >=0.05, no-harm (+-0.03) on an already-calibrated one.

### Changed

- **Early-stop deduplication: the legacy budget-scaled "worsening" detector was REMOVED and the single monotonic stop's default raised `monotonic_decline_patience` 3 -> 7 (benchmark-calibrated).** mlframe carried TWO early-stop curve-shape mechanisms: the OLD budget-scaled "worsening" detector in `UniversalCallback` (threshold `max_iter // coeff`, no plateau reset; ctor params `worsening_enabled`/`worsening_coeff`/`worsening_min_iters`/`worsening_max_iter`, plus the `TrainingBehaviorConfig.early_stop_on_worsening*` knobs and the `PartialFitESWrapper` forwarding) and the NEW fixed-N, plateau-resets `MonotonicDeclineStopper` (`monotonic_decline_patience`, wired byte-identically into the lgb / xgb / cb shims + the neural / sklearn wrappers). The committed benchmark `training/callbacks/_benchmarks/bench_worsening_vs_monotonic_stop.py` (18 lgb fits) settled it: the OLD worsening detector stopped at iteration 499/500 in 18/18 fits (i.e. NEVER fired -- a no-op) AND had the WORST test accuracy; new fixed-N=3 was too aggressive (catastrophic 7-tree stops); N=5 had the best accuracy of the benchmarked {3,5} (ties patience-50, ~3.6x earlier than the full budget); the default is set to a more conservative 7 to avoid false-stops on noisier metrics/models where 5 consecutive declines can occur by chance. So the OLD worsening machinery is deleted entirely (the `maybe_warn_max_iter_hit()` "best_iter hit the budget" diagnostic is retained, decoupled onto its own `UniversalCallback.max_iter` reference -- it never drove a stop), and the canonical `monotonic_decline_patience` default is raised 3 -> 7 at every default site: `TrainingBehaviorConfig`, the neural estimator param + its fit-time fallback, `EarlyStoppingWrapper`, the lgb / xgb shim `.fit()` defaults, and the lgb/xgb/cb monotonic-decline callbacks. Explicitly-passed values are untouched; only DEFAULTS change. Files: `training/callbacks/_callbacks.py` (worsening params/state/methods/call-site removed; `max_iter` diagnostic ref), `training/_partial_fit_es_wrapper.py` + `training/_data_helpers.py` + `training/_trainer_train_and_evaluate.py` (worsening plumbing removed, mono fallback -> 5), `training/_model_configs_behavior.py` (`early_stop_on_worsening*` fields removed, `monotonic_decline_patience` default 5), `training/callbacks/monotonic_decline.py` + `estimators/early_stopping.py` + `training/lgb_shim.py` + `training/xgb_shim.py` + `training/neural/base/__init__.py` + `training/neural/base/_base_fit.py` + `training/neural/_base_callbacks.py` (default 3 -> 7). Bench kept (REJECTED != DELETED). Tests: deleted `test_worsening_streak_detector.py` + `test_worsening_biz_value.py` (superseded by the bench); updated `test_monotonic_decline_boosters.py`, `test_partial_fit_es_wrapper.py`, `test_biz_val_behavior_monotonic_patience.py`, `callbacks/test_callbacks.py`, and the fuzz combo axes/enumerator + `test_fuzz_combo_cross_axis_W11C.py` (worsening axes removed).

### Added

- **Per-iteration full-metric-suite capture for meta-learning / HPO-from-early-observation.** Boosters (lgb/xgb/cb) and neural estimators now optionally record the COMPLETE target-type metric suite on the validation set at each training iteration into `model.iteration_metrics_` (`{iteration -> {metric_name -> float}}`), so downstream meta-learners can predict final holdout performance from the first K iterations and prune bad configs early. The trajectory rides on the early-stopping callback infrastructure that already runs per round/epoch. The existing single-metric `evals_result_` history is untouched (this is additive, richer history). New lean aggregator `mlframe.metrics.compute_all_metrics(y_true, y_score, target_type) -> dict[str, float]` (`metrics/iteration_metrics.py`) delegates to the SAME fast numba kernels the per-model report assembles (`fast_aucs`, `compute_ece_and_brier_decomposition`, `fast_calibration_metrics`, `fast_ice_only`, `fast_log_loss`, `ks_statistic`, `matthews_corrcoef_*`, `brier_skill_score`, `fast_classification_report`, regression `fast_*` block) but WITHOUT the report's plot/figure machinery, so it is cheap enough to call per round; it covers binary/multiclass/multilabel classification + regression and degrades gracefully (NaN, not raise) on single-class val / NaN scores / empty input. Config: `TrainingBehaviorConfig.capture_iteration_metrics` (default OFF for boosters because re-predicting val each round via the booster's native per-iteration prediction is non-trivial; capture is stride-sampled via `iteration_metrics_stride`, always including the final round) and default ON for neural (val predictions are already concatenated each validation-epoch-end, so the only marginal cost is the metric kernel). Measured meta-learning signal: early-iteration (round 15/60) val ROC_AUC rank-predicts the final honest-holdout ROC_AUC across a learning-rate sweep (Spearman ~1.0). Hook points: lgb/xgb shim `fit()` callbacks (`training/callbacks/iteration_metrics.py`), CatBoost via `_setup_early_stopping_callback`, neural `IterationMetricsRecorder` (`neural/_history_recorder.py`). Metric-kernel cost (warm, ms/call): 0.64@5k / 4.86@50k / 31@200k val rows; cProfile harness `metrics/_benchmarks/profile_iteration_metrics_capture.py`. Tests: `tests/metrics/test_iteration_metrics_aggregator.py` (11), `tests/training/test_biz_val_iteration_metrics_capture.py` (4).

### Investigated (no change)

- **Numeric-scaling default stays `scaler_name="standard"` -- the "RobustScaler wins when outliers are present" hypothesis was REJECTED.** `PreprocessingBackendConfig.scaler_name` (default `"standard"`, flows through `create_polarsds_pipeline` -> `_apply_safe_scaler` at `training/pipeline/__init__.py:703`) was probed against `"robust"` (median-center, IQR-scale -- the textbook breakdown-resistant choice) on downstream honest-holdout AUC/R2 for SCALE-SENSITIVE linear models (LogisticRegression / Ridge -- tree models ignore any monotone per-column scaling, so the scaler only moves the needle there). Bench (`training/_benchmarks/bench_scaler_default_standard_vs_robust.py`, 5 scenarios -- 5%-additive-spike contamination (clf+regr) with heterogeneous per-column scales, Cauchy-tail noise, strongly right-skewed lognormal, and a clean Gaussian control -- x 5 seeds, real pipeline fit on train, scored on a disjoint 30% holdout): robust shows **NO scenario-majority win** (robust 8 / standard 12 / tie 5 of 25) and only ~1e-3-magnitude deltas either way; standard wins the clean Gaussian (3/0) and lognormal (4/1) controls, robust edges only the spike-contam-binary cell (3/0) by ~0.0003 AUC. Root cause: once every column is individually normalised, a single global L2 regulariser absorbs the std-vs-IQR scale-family difference -- the honest-holdout metric is near-invariant to the choice, and the spike contamination sits in the data BOTH scalers see (robust's IQR only marginally re-shapes per-column dynamic range, never removing the contaminated rows). A flip needs a scenario-majority robust win WITHOUT a clean-data regression; this bench produces neither, so `"standard"` stays the most-accurate default. The `"robust"` / `"min_max"` / `"abs_max"` options + `robust_q_low`/`robust_q_high` knobs stay reachable per REJECTED != DELETED. Bench kept. Verdict-pinning tests: `tests/training/test_biz_val_training_scaler_default.py::test_biz_val_scaler_default_is_standard` + `::test_biz_val_scaler_robust_still_reachable` + `::test_biz_val_scaler_robust_does_not_dominate_standard_on_contaminated_holdout`. With calibration/metrics/CI (qual-1..12), composite/ensemble (qual-13..19), and now the preprocessing scaler default all saturated, the accessible accuracy/quality-flip surface is broadly mined out -- recommend winding the loop down absent a new default-bearing surface (the imputation strategy default mean-vs-median was likewise benched-and-kept, and the missing-indicator / outlier-detection levers live in the off-limits FE-selection core).
- **Cross-target ensemble residual-correlation member-dedup stays OFF (`ct_ensemble_dedup_enabled=False`) -- flipping it ON is neutral under NNLS stacking and HARMFUL under uniform-mean ensembling.** The opt-in dedup (`training/_composite_target_discovery_config.py:416`, consumed at `core/_phase_composite_post_xt_ensemble/__init__.py:667`) drops near-duplicate members (`|honest-OOF residual Pearson corr| > 0.95`, keeping the lower-OOF-RMSE one) before the cross-target stack/blend, so a redundant cluster can't split + dominate the weight. qual-19 ran the pre-existing committed bench (`training/_benchmarks/bench_ct_ensemble_residual_dedup.py`, NNLS + near-dup-of-strongest pool, 10 seeds: dedup wins 1/10, ~1e-4 deltas) and EXTENDED it to 3 scenarios (`bench_ct_ensemble_residual_dedup_scenarios.py`: `nnls_dup` NNLS n_dup=4 jitter=0.02 / `mean_dup` uniform-mean same pool / `nnls_tight` NNLS n_dup=8 jitter=0.005, x 10 seeds, dedup keyed on the OOF-residual surface the stacker fits, test RMSE on a disjoint 50% holdout). Result is 0/3 scenarios ON (majority-of-scenarios rule): NNLS is NEUTRAL (`nnls_dup` mean RMSE full 1.30454 vs dedup 1.30459, 1/10; `nnls_tight` 1.30452 vs 1.30453, 1/10) because the stacker already learns the optimal combined weight regardless of how near-duplicate members split it; and the uniform-`mean` case -- where redundant members mechanically split the average weight and dedup SHOULD win -- is actively HARMFUL (`mean_dup` full 1.44867 vs dedup 1.48968, +2.83%, dedup wins 0/10), because the redundant cluster's average is a LOWER-VARIANCE estimate of the shared strong signal than any single survivor, so pruning it RAISES the uniform-mean variance. The default OFF is the most-accurate default; the knob + corr-threshold stay reachable per REJECTED != DELETED. With the post-discovery aggregation/blend path (qual-13/14/16/17), the discovery-selection gate (qual-18), and now this ensemble member-pruning lever all resolved-or-confirmed, the composite/ensemble surface is saturated (the remaining opt-in gates -- `stacking_aware_gate_enabled`, `use_wilcoxon_gate`, `use_stacked_discovery_residual`, `require_beats_raw_baseline` -- carry their own measured rationale for defaulting OFF, documented at `_composite_target_discovery_config_base.py:443-460`). Bench kept (REJECTED != DELETED). Verdict-pinning tests: `tests/training/composite/test_biz_val_training_composite_dedup.py::test_biz_val_ct_ensemble_dedup_does_not_help_nnls_or_mean_on_redundant_pool` + `::test_biz_val_ct_ensemble_dedup_default_is_off`.
- **Cross-target ensemble output-calibration method stays `isotonic` (default) -- the "sigmoid is more robust at small OOF" hypothesis was REJECTED.** The cross-target ensemble's opt-in post-hoc output recalibration (`OutputCalibrator` / `cross_target_calibration_method`) fits a monotone map of the OOF-blended prediction onto the OOF truth on a SMALL holdout (default `oof_holdout_frac=0.2` of train, often only a few hundred rows). The Niculescu-Mizil & Caruana prior says free-form isotonic (PAV, ~n step parameters) should overfit a small calibration set while the 2-parameter Platt/sigmoid generalises better at small n, suggesting the default should flip to sigmoid at small OOF. Tested on an isolated honest-holdout shootout (`composite/ensemble/_benchmarks/bench_calibration_method.py`, 2 monotone-miscalibration scenarios -- S-shape tanh-squash + sqrt-saturation -- x OOF sizes {60,150,400,1500} x 8 seeds, calibrator fit on OOF and scored on an INDEPENDENT 4000-row holdout): isotonic beats sigmoid on holdout RMSE in **8/8 seeds in EVERY cell, including the smallest n_oof=60** (s_shape: iso 0.4417 vs sig 0.8998 vs raw 0.5908; saturating: iso 0.4250 vs sig 1.1508 vs raw 0.6445). The OutputCalibrator's `sigmoid` is an OLS-on-centred-logit affine-link map (not a true cross-entropy Platt fit), so it forces an S-shape the regression miscalibrations don't have and is actually WORSE than the raw blend at small n; isotonic's free-form PAV tracks the real distortion and the gap WIDENS as OOF shrinks. The default `isotonic` is the most-accurate map across all OOF sizes; no flip. Bench kept (REJECTED != DELETED). Verdict-pinning tests: `tests/training/test_ensemble_output_calibration.py::test_biz_val_isotonic_beats_sigmoid_on_small_oof_holdout` (3 OOF sizes) + `::test_default_calibration_method_is_isotonic`.
- **`fast_log_loss` probability-clip eps stays dtype-dependent (`eps=np.finfo(y_pred.dtype).eps`) -- investigated and KEPT, not a bug.** The hypothesis that the float32 default clip (1.19e-7) inflates / biases log-loss and should flip to a fixed float64 eps (2.22e-16) was tested on an overconfident-wrong synthetic (conf in {1e-8,1e-10}, 5% wrong rows, n=100k, 5 seeds x 2 scenarios; honest metric = `|fast_log_loss - sklearn_log_loss(float64)|`). The legacy float32 eps IS mildly optimistic (ml32 ~0.78 vs honest ~0.90), but the proposed fix is strictly WORSE: forcing the float64 eps onto float32 input OVERSHOOTS to ~1.34 (+48%), because float32 cannot represent probabilities in `(1 - 1.19e-7, 1)` -- a confident `1 - 1e-8` collapses to exactly `1.0` on the cast, and clipping that at 2.22e-16 charges `-log(2.22e-16) ~= 36` per unrepresentable-near-1 confident-wrong row instead of the intended `-log(1e-8) ~= 18`. The bias direction flips with the eps choice and float32 simply cannot carry the near-1 information, so the dtype-matched eps is the least-wrong floor; the float64 path already reproduces sklearn's effectively-no-clip value exactly. No unambiguously-better dtype-independent default exists, so the default is unchanged (pass an explicit `eps` to override). Bench: `metrics/_benchmarks/bench_log_loss_dtype_eps_qual11.py`. Reject-pinning tests: `tests/metrics/test_biz_val_metrics_log_loss_dtype_eps.py`.

### Added

- **Monotonic strict-decline early stop hardened across all boosters: single canonical knob `monotonic_decline_patience`, best-iteration rollback for XGBoost, unknown-metric-direction SKIP, and the legacy budget-scaled detector defaulted OFF.** Four correctness/safety fixes from a two-agent adversarial review of the default-on monotonic stop (the shared fixed-N, plateau-resets `MonotonicDeclineStopper` driving lgb / xgb / cb / mlp + the sklearn wrapper). (1) **XGB best-iteration rollback (D1):** unlike LightGBM's `EarlyStopException` (which truncates the returned booster), XGBoost's `after_iteration`-returns-True kept the FULL booster including the post-best overfit tail, and `XGBModel.predict` then scored every round. The xgb callback now tracks the best iteration and on stop stamps `best_iteration` (+ `best_score`) on the booster via `set_attr`, so `_get_iteration_range` truncates prediction to `best_iteration + 1` -- predict now matches the best-iteration prediction, not the overfit tail. (2) **Unknown-metric direction SKIP (D5):** when a monitored metric name is not in `metrics_registry` AND no explicit `mode=` is given, `_resolve_mode` no longer GUESSES `"min"` (which silently inverts the rule for a higher-is-better custom metric -- stopping at/near the best, or never) -- it returns a SKIP sentinel that DISABLES the monotonic stop for that fit with a one-time WARNING. Common native booster loss aliases that were previously unknown (`l2`, `l1`, `binary_logloss`, `multi_logloss`, `binary_error`, `multi_error`, `error`, `fair`, `tweedie`, `gamma`, `poisson`; `auc_mu` as higher-is-better) are now registered so they resolve instead of self-disabling. (3) **Single detector by default (D2):** the OLD budget-scaled "worsening" detector in `UniversalCallback` (threshold `max_iter // coeff`, no plateau reset) is now `worsening_enabled=False` by default so it no longer double-stops the same fit alongside the canonical `MonotonicDeclineStopper`; the legacy detector is retained for opt-in parity benchmarks (`worsening_enabled=True`). (4) **The missing off-switch (C1/C2):** `monotonic_decline_patience: int | None = 3` is now a first-class `TrainingBehaviorConfig` field, threaded through `_trainer_train_and_evaluate` into the lgb / xgb shim `.fit()` kwarg and the CatBoost `callback_params` (mirroring the `early_stop_on_worsening*` plumbing) -- `monotonic_decline_patience=None` disables the stop everywhere so the booster trains its full iteration cap. Plateau-resets semantics are unchanged: a new global best, an exact plateau, OR a bounce-up all reset the strict-decline streak; only an uninterrupted run of `patience` strictly-worse steps fires. NOTE (D4): on a PATHOLOGICAL val curve of 3 strictly-consecutive declines that later recovers, fixed-N=3 does false-stop before the recovery -- an accepted-for-now aggressiveness pinned by a regression test; an adaptive noise-gate is deferred. Files: `training/callbacks/monotonic_decline.py` (xgb best-iter stamping, `_UNKNOWN_DIRECTION` sentinel + SKIP wiring in all three backends), `training/callbacks/_callbacks.py` (`worsening_enabled` default False), `training/metrics_registry.py` (booster loss-alias directions), `training/_model_configs_behavior.py` (`monotonic_decline_patience` field), `training/_trainer_train_and_evaluate.py` (threading). Tests: `tests/training/test_monotonic_decline_boosters.py` (xgb best-iter stamp + biz_value predict==best-iter; unknown-metric SKIP for lgb/cb; D2 single-detector default; D4 noise-curve baselines), `tests/training/test_biz_val_behavior_monotonic_patience.py` (None disables -> full cap).
- **Monotonic strict-decline early stop now GOVERNS CatBoost training too (`CBMonotonicDeclineStop`, default ON at patience=3) -- completing the cb / lgb / xgb / mlp set.** The shared fixed-N, plateau-resets `MonotonicDeclineStopper` (stop once the monitored val metric STRICTLY worsens for N consecutive iterations since the global best; a new best / plateau / bounce-up resets the streak) already drove the LightGBM + XGBoost shims and the Lightning MLP; CatBoost was previously SKIPPED on the (now-refuted) belief that its Python callback hook could not veto continuation. Verified against catboost 1.2.10: `CatBoostClassifier/Regressor.fit(..., callbacks=[obj])` calls `obj.after_iteration(info)` once per iteration with `info.metrics` keyed by dataset (`"learn"` / `"validation"`) then metric, and returning `False` stops training (confirmed: a callback returning `False` at iteration 7 yields `tree_count_ == 7`). `CBMonotonicDeclineStop` wraps the shared stopper and is auto-injected alongside `CatBoostCallback` in `_setup_early_stopping_callback` for the cb branch, gated on a runtime `catboost_callbacks_supported()` probe so older builds fall back gracefully to the native `od_wait` detector. Default-on at 3 per "enable corrective mechanisms by default"; `monotonic_decline_patience=None` in `callback_params` disables it. Cross-model business value on an overfit-prone noisy-label synthetic (clean held-out test AUC): lgb stops at 34 trees vs 400 baseline (+0.0176 AUC), xgb 92 vs 400 (+0.0091), cb 35 vs 400 (-0.0025, noise), mlp 8 vs 12 epochs (best-epoch loss preserved). Files: `training/callbacks/monotonic_decline.py` (`CBMonotonicDeclineStop`, `catboost_callbacks_supported`), `training/_data_helpers.py` (`_setup_early_stopping_callback` cb wiring + `monotonic_decline_patience` pull). Tests: `tests/training/test_biz_val_monotonic_decline_cross_model.py` (quantitative stops-earlier + no-test-regression for ALL of lgb/xgb/cb/mlp), `tests/training/callbacks/test_callbacks.py::TestCatBoostMonotonicDeclineWiring` (injection, default-on, disable-via-None, fires-on-worsening, plateau-resets).
- **`fast_adjusted_r2_score` (`mlframe.metrics`): degrees-of-freedom-corrected R^2 for goodness-of-fit on the fitting sample.** Plain R^2 is upward-biased on the data the model was fit on -- it never decreases when predictors are added, so it overstates explained variance whenever the predictor count `p` is non-trivial relative to `n` (a junk feature still nudges R^2 up). `fast_adjusted_r2_score(y_true, y_pred, n_predictors)` applies the standard Wherry / Ezekiel correction `1 - (1 - R^2) * (n - 1) / (n - p - 1)` on top of the existing `fast_r2_score` kernel; `p <= 0` returns plain R^2 unchanged and `p >= n - 1` (non-positive denominator, the perfect-interpolation regime where R^2 is meaningless) returns NaN. Known-ground-truth bench (`metrics/_benchmarks/bench_adjusted_r2.py`, true population R^2 exactly 0.5 for `y = x0 + noise` with x0,noise ~ N(0,1), OLS fit on x0 plus (p-1) pure-noise predictors, 7 seeds): in the regime adjusted R^2 exists for (p/n >= 0.1) adjusted is closer to the true 0.5 in 27/35 cells and mean `|estimate - true|` drops from 0.138 (plain) to 0.080 (adjusted, 42% reduction) -- the largest win at small-n/large-p (n=50, p=20: plain R^2 ~0.71 vs adjusted ~0.51 at true 0.5). At small p/n the two converge (wash, both ~0.022, no harm). Ships as a NEW function rather than a default flip on `fast_r2_score`: the two answer different questions and adjusted R^2 requires a predictor count the plain `(y_true, y_pred)` signature has no way to supply. Files: `metrics/regression/_regression_extras.py` (`fast_adjusted_r2_score`), `metrics/regression/__init__.py` + `metrics/core.py` (re-export). Bench: `metrics/_benchmarks/bench_adjusted_r2.py`. Tests: `tests/metrics/regression/test_regression_extras.py::test_adjusted_r2_*` (textbook-formula parity, p=0 -> plain, perfect-fit, degenerate-denominator NaN, penalises-below-plain) + `tests/metrics/regression/test_biz_val_metrics_adjusted_r2.py` (closer-to-true majority win when p matters; no-harm convergence at small p/n).
- **`auc_variance` / `auc_ci` (`mlframe.evaluation`): closed-form (DeLong) standard error + confidence interval for a single-sample ROC-AUC.** mlframe shipped the PAIRED DeLong test (`delong_test`) but had no single-sample AUC uncertainty estimate -- the de-facto path was bootstrapping `roc_auc_score` (the qual-5 BCa interval). `auc_variance(y_true, score)` returns `{auc, variance, se}` from the DeLong / Hanley-McNeil structural-component estimator (Sun & Xu 2014 O(n log n) form, `Var = Var(v10)/n_pos + Var(v01)/n_neg` with `ddof=1` sample variances, tie-aware via midranks); `auc_ci(y_true, score, alpha=0.05, method="delong")` wraps it as a logit-scale Wald interval that stays strictly inside (0, 1) -- the standard remedy for a raw-scale Wald interval over-shooting 1.0 when AUC is near the ceiling. `method="bootstrap"` routes to the stratified BCa `bootstrap_metric` for callers who want a fully non-parametric interval. The closed-form path is instant, deterministic, and RNG-free. The `delong_test` structural-component computation was refactored into a shared `_auc_structural_components` helper (behaviour-preserving; the paired DeLong tests pass unchanged). Honest bench verdict (`evaluation/_benchmarks/bench_auc_ci_delong_vs_bootstrap.py`, ground truth = Monte-Carlo SD of AUC over 4000 independent test draws of a known normal-shift model, 5 scenarios sweeping small-n / high-AUC x 8 seeds): against the qual-5 BCa bootstrap the DeLong SE is a STATISTICAL WASH on `|SE - truth-SD|` (DeLong closer in 22/40 scenario x seed cells, ~55%; per-scenario 3/8 .. 6/8) and coverage is essentially TIED (auc0.97/n150 both 0.943; auc0.97/n400 DeLong gap 0.013 vs bootstrap 0.017). So DeLong is NOT measurably more accurate than the bootstrap for AUC uncertainty -- its value is being closed-form / instant / deterministic, which is why it ships as a new function (DeLong is the internal default of `auc_ci`; there was no prior single-sample AUC-CI default to regress against), NOT as a default flip on `bootstrap_metric`. Files: `evaluation/bootstrap.py` (`auc_variance`, `auc_ci`, `_auc_structural_components`), `evaluation/__init__.py` (re-export). Bench: `evaluation/_benchmarks/bench_auc_ci_delong_vs_bootstrap.py`. Tests: `tests/evaluation/test_biz_val_evaluation_auc_ci.py` (DeLong SE tracks truth at least as well as bootstrap on auc=0.85/n=200; logit-Wald CI near-nominal coverage + stays < 1.0 at the ceiling; degenerate / non-binary guards; bootstrap-method parity).

- **MRMR `fe_fast_search` master toggle (default ON) -- a substantially faster FE search that still recovers the signal, replacing the exhaustive tail with a quality-preserving fast profile.** On the two canonical n=100k interaction synthetics (`y = a**2/b + f/5 + log(c)*sin(d)` and the warped/down-weighted `y = 0.2*a**2/b + f/5 + log(c*2)*sin(d/3)`) a warm fit dropped from ~130s / ~100s to under 60s each (CPU, RAM-contended dev box), with BOTH interactions still recovered (an engineered feature jointly covering (a,b) AND one covering (c,d), the warped (c,d) captured via the conditional-gate composite) and the Ridge-holdout MAE within tolerance -- in fact BETTER on both (CASE1 0.04995 unchanged; CASE2 0.0984 -> 0.086), since the dropped passes mostly add noise columns on these targets. When ON AND the user left a sub-knob at its package default (an explicit value always wins), `fit()` applies a fast profile for the duration of the fit and restores every knob in `finally` (clone / pickle / repeated-fit constructor-arg stability, mirroring `fe_auto`): `fe_max_steps` 2->1 (skip the second augmented-pool pass whose only product is FUSING two already-found half-composites into one column -- cosmetic for a linear/tree downstream, the two halves give the identical fit; the single biggest lever); `fe_pair_prewarp_enable`/`fe_stability_vote_enable`/`fe_escalation_underdelivery_enable` -> False (drop the per-operand learned pre-warp sweep + the post-discovery cross-fold confirmation / under-delivery escalation passes); `fe_check_pairs_subsample_n` -> a kernel_tuning_cache-resolved screen-n (HW/size aware via `mrmr_fast_search_screen_n`, safe 90_000 fallback -- the MI/CMI pair screen is rank-stable under subsampling and the FINAL survivor columns are replayed at FULL n). The conditional-gate DETECTION scan (`cheap_conditional_gate_scan` / `hybrid_conditional_gate_fe_with_recipes`) gained a `subsample_n` param wired from the same fast screen-n (tau + MI ranking are rank-stable; the gate recipe replays at full n). It is SELECTION-ALTERING (approximately, not bit-, equal) and therefore behind this clearly-named toggle: `fe_fast_search=False` restores the exhaustive legacy search BYTE-IDENTICALLY (the override block is skipped entirely). Files: `mrmr/_mrmr_class.py` (ctor flag + `_apply_fast_search_profile` / `_fast_search_default_subsample_n` + fit-time apply/restore), `_conditional_gate_fe.py` (scan `subsample_n`), `_mrmr_fit_impl/_fit_impl_core.py` (gate-scan subsample wiring), `_feature_engineering_pairs/_pairs_core.py` + `feature_engineering.py` (`_rebuild_full_survivor_col` engineered-operand support -- a prereq bug fix, below). Tests: `tests/feature_selection/test_biz_value_mrmr_fast_search.py` (both cases recover the signal + MAE within 10% of exhaustive + fast path materially faster; toggle restores knobs).

- **Bug fix (subsample path): `check_prospective_fe_pairs` survivor-rebuild crashed with `KeyError` on an ENGINEERED operand when `fe_check_pairs_subsample_n` was active.** At FE step k>1 the operand pool carries the prior step's engineered columns (var indices beyond the raw `original_cols`), but `_rebuild_full_survivor_col` resolved operands ONLY via `original_cols[var_idx]`, raising `KeyError` for any engineered operand -- so the subsample screen path (previously never exercised at n < 200k by default) was broken for multi-step FE. `_rebuild_full_survivor_col` now mirrors the MI-sweep operand semantics: an engineered operand is resolved by NAME from the full-n continuous store (`engineered_operand_values[cols[var_idx]]`) or, as a fallback, from the augmented frame, before falling back to the raw-position lookup. This is the prerequisite that lets `fe_fast_search` subsample the pair screen safely. Files: `feature_engineering.py` (`_rebuild_full_survivor_col` + new `cols` / `engineered_operand_values` params), `_feature_engineering_pairs/_pairs_core.py` (call site passes them). Covered by `test_biz_value_mrmr_fast_search.py` (the fast path exercises the subsample survivor-rebuild on the multi-step pool).

- **`discover_structure` now attaches a permutation-test p-value to each discovered relation (`DiscoveredRelation.p_value`, `significance_n_perm=100` default).** The detectors' own 12-perm gate is enough for a yes/no decision but too coarse for a reportable confidence; since `discover_structure` is an offline EDA call (not the hot fit path) it can afford a deeper null. For each discovered relation it reconstructs the engineered column (via the operators' `apply_*` replay) and computes `(1 + #{perm_mi >= obs_mi}) / (1 + n_perm)` against permuted-y nulls, surfacing it in the description (`"... lift 6.4x, p<0.005"`). Significance testing does NOT create false discovery (a noise frame stays empty); `significance_n_perm=0` disables it (`p_value` is then `nan` and the p is omitted). Files: `feature_selection/structure_discovery.py`. Tests: `test_biz_val_structure_discovery.py` (strong p on genuine gcd structure, off-switch omits p, noise frame stays empty with the deeper null on).

- **`discover_structure(X, y) -> StructureReport`: a standalone public EDA / structure-discovery tool** (`from mlframe.feature_selection import discover_structure`). Surfaces the hidden DISCRETE structural relationships the four shipped FE detectors find -- turning them from silent feature-emitters into a user-facing insight tool. It runs the four detectors (pairwise/n-way MODULAR `(a+b) mod m` / parity / hidden period, integer-LATTICE `gcd`/`lcm`/`bitwise_and`, row-ARGMAX, conditional-GATE regime-switch `c>tau ? a : b` / masked-interaction `1[c>tau]*a`) on `(X, y)` WITHOUT a full MRMR fit, and returns a ranked, human-readable `StructureReport` of `DiscoveredRelation` entries -- each carrying the relationship `kind`, source column NAMES, the detected parameter (modulus `m` / threshold `tau` / `None`), the engineered-column MI vs y, the `lift` over the best-existing-op baseline, and a one-line description (e.g. `"y depends on gcd(price, quantity)  [MI 0.97, lift 6.4x]"`, `"regime switch: y ~ (discount if tenure>0.398 else base)  [MI 1.91]"`). `str(report)` / `.summary()` print the ranked block; an empty report renders `"no discrete structural relationships detected"`. It REUSES each detector's scan + permutation-null + best-existing-op gate verbatim (no re-implemented detection / MI / null logic), so the 0-false-discovery guarantee is INHERITED: a smooth / linear / noise frame returns an EMPTY report. Accepts a pandas DataFrame (or numpy ndarray, wrapped with positional `f0,f1,...` names) and a 1D classification OR regression target (continuous y is quantile-binned internally via the operators' shared `bin_y_for_class_mi`); a 2D / multi-target y skips with a warning (mirroring the operators). Budget-guarded (`max_int_cols`, the operators' own relevance-pruned caps); runs in ~1-2s on n=2000, p<=20. A fitted `MRMR` additionally exposes a near-free read-only `discovered_structure_` property assembled from the frozen FE recipe metadata (kind / src_names / op / modulus / tau) the operators already emitted during `fit` (MI/lift are `nan` there -- the fit did not freeze the scan MI; for those, call the standalone `discover_structure`). Files: new `feature_selection/structure_discovery.py`, exported from `feature_selection/__init__.py`; `mrmr/_mrmr_class.py` (`discovered_structure_` property). Tests: `tests/feature_selection/test_biz_val_structure_discovery.py` (biz_value: gcd-classification + gcd-regression + modular + regime-switch + argmax surface the right relation with the right columns/parameter clearing a measured MI/lift floor; specificity: linear + noise frames yield 0 discoveries; robustness: 2D-y skip+warn, tiny frame no-crash, numpy fallback; human-readable: `str` carries columns + kind; the MRMR recipe accessor).

### Changed

- **Composite multi-base discovery gate `multi_base_min_marginal_rmse_gain` default flips from 0.02 to 0.005; the legacy 2% gate stays reachable via the explicit kwarg.** The forward-stepwise auto-promotion (`forward_stepwise_multi_base`, default ON via `multi_base_enabled`) upgrades a single-base `linear_residual` composite to `linear_residual_multi` by greedily ADDING bases whose marginal relative CV-RMSE gain clears this threshold. At 2% the gate stopped early and left genuinely-helpful WEAK orthogonal bases out of the discovered target. qual-18 measured this on a leakage-safe honest holdout: the gate selects bases on TRAIN only (internal TimeSeriesSplit/KFold CV + the paired-fold majority gate), the joint-OLS composite target is fit on TRAIN, a LightGBM model is trained on the composite target, and its inverted prediction is scored on a DISJOINT 40% holdout the discovery never saw (`discovery/_benchmarks/bench_multibase_min_marginal_gain.py`, n=3000, 3 scenarios x up to 10 seeds, thresholds {0.005, 0.01, 0.02, 0.05}). 0.005 beats the 0.02 incumbent on honest-holdout RMSE in **20/20 non-tied seeds** across the two additive multi-base DGPs (`three_real_bases` mean 0.7363 vs 0.8749 = -15.8%, 10/10; `graded_weak_real` 0.6761 vs 0.7786 = -13.2%, 10/10) by admitting the weak-but-real base the 2% gate dropped (3.0 vs 2.0 bases kept), and on the single-dominant-base + noise-decoy DGP it EXACT-TIES the incumbent (10/10 ties, 1 base kept) because the paired-fold majority gate rejects the noise bases independent of the relative threshold -- so there is zero honest-holdout regression where extra bases would hurt. The flip is replay-safe: the `_fit.py` read site uses `getattr(self.config, "multi_base_min_marginal_rmse_gain", 0.02)`, so pre-field pickled configs replay with the legacy 0.02 while only newly-constructed configs default to 0.005. Set `multi_base_min_marginal_rmse_gain=0.02` for the conservative gate (highly-correlated pools / very small n_train). Files: `training/_composite_target_discovery_config_base.py` (default + docstring). Bench: `training/composite/discovery/_benchmarks/bench_multibase_min_marginal_gain.py` (+ `_results/multibase_min_marginal_gain.json`). Tests: `tests/training/composite/test_biz_val_training_composite_discovery.py::test_biz_val_composite_discovery_multi_base_min_marginal_gain_default_is_0_005` + `::test_biz_val_composite_discovery_low_gain_gate_beats_legacy_on_additive_holdout` + `::test_biz_val_composite_discovery_low_gain_gate_no_regression_on_dominant_base` + `::test_biz_val_composite_discovery_legacy_002_gate_still_reachable`.
- **`BaggedCompositeEstimator` trimmed-mean default `trim_fraction` flips from 0.1 to 0.2 (the robust knee); the legacy 0.1 stays reachable via the explicit kwarg.** qual-14 introduced the trimmed-mean aggregator with a 10% trim. qual-17 swept the trim fraction over {0.0(=mean), 0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30} across more contamination levels (clean Gaussian / heavy-tail Student-t(df=2) / 5%, 15%, 30% large-spike outlier contamination) x 7 seeds, honest-holdout RMSE+MAE vs the noiseless truth (`training/composite/_benchmarks/bench_bagging_trim_fraction_qual17.py`, DecisionTree base, 25 members). Honest RMSE decreased monotonically with the trim on every contaminated scenario; trim=0.2 wins the per-seed honest RMSE on 28/35 seed-scenario cells vs the 0.1 incumbent -- 7/7 on EVERY contaminated scenario (~5-9% RMSE drop: contam_30 mean RMSE 2.0838 at 0.1 -> 1.9763 at 0.2) -- for an immaterial clean-data cost (clean mean RMSE 1.6717 -> 1.6765, +0.29%; deep 20-seed clean check 1.6107 -> 1.6136, +0.18%, 0.2 still wins 7/20 seeds). The grid's aggregate optimum sat at the 0.30 edge (sum-RMSE 62.89 vs 0.2's 63.46, within 0.9%), but the edge value is near-median and risks contamination-overfit, so the default is set to the 0.2 KNEE which captures essentially all the contamination benefit without sitting on the grid boundary. The flip is replay-safe: pre-qual-14 pickles lack the `trim_fraction` attribute (and `aggregation`, so they replay as the legacy plain mean); qual-14-or-later pickles carry their stored `trim_fraction`; only newly-constructed ensembles default to 0.2. `aggregation="mean"` / `"median"` and any explicit `trim_fraction` remain available. Files: `training/composite/bagging.py` (`BaggedCompositeEstimator.__init__` default + docstring/comment). Bench: `training/composite/_benchmarks/bench_bagging_trim_fraction_qual17.py`. Tests: `tests/training/test_biz_val_training_composite_bagging.py::test_biz_val_bagging_trim_fraction_default_is_0_2` + `::test_biz_val_bagging_trim_0_2_beats_0_1_on_heavy_contamination` + `::test_biz_val_bagging_trim_0_2_near_0_1_on_clean_data`.
- **`OutputCalibrator(method="sigmoid")` now fits a proper MAXIMUM-LIKELIHOOD Platt map (`sigmoid_fit="platt"`, new default); the legacy OLS-on-centred-logit surrogate is kept opt-in as `sigmoid_fit="ols_logit"` for replay.** qual-15 found the cross-target ensemble's `sigmoid` recalibration option was NOT a Platt fit -- it regressed `logit(t_norm)` on the normalised prediction by ORDINARY least squares, an objective dominated by the near-asymptote clipping, so it barely improved on (and at small OOF was WORSE than) the raw blend. qual-16 fixes the OPTION: `_fit_sigmoid` now minimises the weighted binomial cross-entropy of the [0,1]-normalised truth (`sigmoid(A*z+B)`, the objective Platt scaling is defined by) via a 2-parameter Newton solve. On an honest-holdout shootout (`composite/ensemble/_benchmarks/bench_sigmoid_platt_vs_ols_logit.py`, 3 scenarios -- genuine logistic miscalibration of a probability target / over-confident probability blend / regression tanh-S-shape -- x OOF sizes {60,150,400} x 8 seeds, fit on OOF and scored on a DISJOINT 4000-row holdout) the MLE Platt map beats the OLS-logit surrogate on holdout RMSE in **8/8 seeds in EVERY one of the 9 cells (72/72 seeds)**, beats the raw blend 8/8 on the logistic-miscalibration target at every size (and 5-8/8 on the over-confident target), and is strictly better than the broken OLS-logit map even on the regression S-shape where isotonic ultimately wins. The headline `method` default stays `isotonic` (qual-15 verdict: isotonic is the most-accurate map on the regression miscalibrations the cross-target ensemble actually leaves behind); this change makes the `sigmoid` OPTION actually useful when a user opts into it for a genuinely-logistic probability output. Replay-safe: pre-qual-16 fitted pickles lack the `sigmoid_fit` attribute, so the predict path (which never reads it) is byte-identical and any re-fit getattr-defaults to `"platt"`; only newly-constructed calibrators see the new fit. `fit_output_calibrator` gained a pass-through `sigmoid_fit` arg. Files: `training/composite/ensemble/_calibration.py` (`OutputCalibrator.__init__` `sigmoid_fit` param, `_fit_sigmoid` MLE rewrite, `_fit_sigmoid_ols_logit` legacy path, `_platt_mle_1d` Newton solver, `export()` records the fit). Bench: `training/composite/ensemble/_benchmarks/bench_sigmoid_platt_vs_ols_logit.py`. Tests: `tests/training/test_ensemble_output_calibration.py::test_biz_val_platt_sigmoid_beats_ols_logit_and_raw_on_holdout` (3 OOF sizes) + `::test_default_sigmoid_fit_is_platt` + `::test_sigmoid_platt_is_monotone_and_rejects_bad_fit` + `::test_ols_logit_sigmoid_fit_still_reachable_for_replay`.

- **`BaggedCompositeEstimator` (`mlframe.training.composite.bagging`) collapses the per-member regression-prediction matrix with a 10% TRIMMED MEAN by default (`aggregation="trimmed_mean"`, `trim_fraction=0.1`); pass `aggregation="mean"` for the legacy plain mean or `aggregation="median"` for the maximally-robust aggregator.** The legacy `members.mean(axis=0)` is the Gaussian-MLE-optimal blend, but a single wild bootstrap member (a pathological resample) skews it; the trimmed mean drops the symmetric `trim_fraction` extreme members per row before averaging, gaining median-like robustness on heavy-tailed / outlier-contaminated targets while converging back to the plain mean as the trim or the contamination goes to 0. Measured on `training/composite/_benchmarks/bench_bagging_aggregation_qual14.py` (DecisionTree base, 25 members; 3 scenarios -- Student-t(df=2) heavy-tail noise / 5%-outlier-contamination / clean Gaussian -- x 7 seeds = 21 cells; honest-holdout RMSE + MAE vs noiseless truth): trimmed-mean beats the plain mean on outlier-contamination 7/7 seeds for both RMSE and MAE (mean rel RMSE -4.7%) and on heavy-tail 5/7 for both (-2.8%, more wins than the median's 4/7), while on clean Gaussian data it costs only +0.42% mean rel RMSE (worst +1.16%) -- a near-tie, not a regression. The maximally-robust median wins outlier-contamination too but regresses clean-data MAE (0/7), so the trimmed mean is the efficiency-preserving default. The flip is replay-safe: pre-flip pickles lack the `aggregation` / `trim_fraction` attributes, so `predict` resolves them via `getattr(self, "aggregation", "mean")` and reproduces the legacy plain-mean blend byte-identically; only newly-constructed ensembles default to the trimmed mean. `predict_std` / `predict_interval_epistemic` are unchanged (the epistemic spread is a Gaussian mean+std notion, independent of the point-estimate aggregator). Files: `training/composite/bagging.py` (`BaggedCompositeEstimator.__init__` `aggregation` / `trim_fraction` params + default flip, `predict` dispatch). Bench: `training/composite/_benchmarks/bench_bagging_aggregation_qual14.py`. Tests: `tests/training/test_biz_val_training_composite_bagging.py`.

- **`_ChainEnsemble` (`mlframe.training`) blends per-member class probabilities with a GEOMETRIC mean / log-average (logarithmic opinion pool) by default (`proba_aggregation="geometric"`); pass `proba_aggregation="arithmetic"` for the legacy linear-pool mean.** Combining diverse members' probabilities by arithmetic mean (`stacked.mean(axis=0)`) is the linear opinion pool; the geometric mean `exp(mean_m log p)` (renormalised to the simplex) is the logarithmic opinion pool, which is the proper-scoring-rule-optimal blend for diverse members under log-loss. Measured on `training/composite/ensemble/_benchmarks/bench_proba_aggregation_arith_vs_geom.py` (synthetic K-member binary/3/4-class ensembles on a known target, honest-holdout NLL / Brier / ECE, 8 scenarios x 7 seeds = 56 cells, varying member diversity / member calibration temperature / K / class balance, plus 3 adversarial scenarios injecting confidently-wrong rogue members): geometric wins NLL in 50/56 cells (89%, 7 of 8 scenarios), Brier 50/56, ECE 53/56 (8/8 scenarios on the per-scenario seed-mean), cutting per-scenario mean NLL e.g. 0.286 -> 0.222 (diverse-calibrated binary), 0.168 -> 0.078 (overconfident members), 0.896 -> 0.781 (low-K high-diversity 4-class) -- improving Brier and ECE in aggregate, not just NLL. The single regime where arithmetic wins is extreme rogue contamination (40% of members confidently wrong), the known log-pool fragility, which is exactly why the linear pool is retained as the opt-in. The flip is replay-safe: pre-flip pickles lack the `proba_aggregation` attribute, so `predict_proba` resolves it via `getattr(self, "proba_aggregation", "arithmetic")` and reproduces the legacy arithmetic blend byte-identically; only newly-constructed ensembles default to geometric. Multilabel-chain output (each `(N, K)` column is an independent `P(label_j=1)` that does not sum to 1 across labels) is renormalised per-label against its own complement geometric mean, never across labels. A shared `aggregate_member_probas(stacked, method, simplex=...)` helper centralises both pools. Files: `training/_classif_helpers.py` (`aggregate_member_probas`, `_ChainEnsemble.__init__` / `predict_proba`, `_build_classifier_chain_ensemble`). Bench: `training/composite/ensemble/_benchmarks/bench_proba_aggregation_arith_vs_geom.py`. Tests: `tests/training/test_biz_val_classif_proba_aggregation.py`.

- **`compute_fairness_metrics` (`metric_std` column) and `robust_mlperf_metric` (the dispersion penalty) in `mlframe.metrics` now use the Bessel-corrected sample std (`ddof=1`, default); pass `ddof=0` for the legacy population-std behaviour.** The fairness `metric_std` is the across-subgroup dispersion of model performance -- it estimates the population dispersion of subgroup performance from a small SAMPLE of K subgroups (typically 2-10: gender, age decile, region, ...). numpy's default `ddof=0` (population std) is the DOWNWARD-biased estimator at small K, so it systematically UNDER-reports subgroup disparity -- making a model look fairer than it is, and under-penalising it inside `robust_mlperf_metric`. `ddof=1` (Bessel) is the unbiased sample-variance / less-biased sample-std convention already used throughout mlframe's feature-engineering code (`hurst`, `grouped`, `spatial`, `ensemble_features`) and by pandas / R / statistics texts. Measured on `metrics/_benchmarks/bench_fairness_std_bessel.py` (K subgroups drawn from N(mu, sigma_true^2) with KNOWN sigma_true, scenarios K in {2,3,4,6,10} x 8 seeds, 4000 Monte-Carlo redraws/cell): the SIGNED std bias (the systematic under-estimation) is cut ~2.2-2.7x in EVERY scenario (K2 -0.0217 -> -0.0100, K3 -0.0138 -> -0.0057, K4 -0.0204 -> -0.0080, K6 -0.0105 -> -0.0039, K10 -0.0037 -> -0.0012), the variance estimand becomes essentially unbiased (ddof=1 ~ 0 vs ddof=0 systematically negative; ddof=1 wins signed-|bias| 5/5 scenarios for both std and variance), and ddof=1 also wins the noisier `|estimate - sigma_true|` metric in 33/40 (scenario x seed) cells (majority at every scenario). A single in-spec group now yields NaN std (n_finite <= ddof) rather than a spurious 0. Files: `metrics/_fairness_metrics.py` (`compute_fairness_metrics` / `robust_mlperf_metric` `ddof` kwarg + default flip). Bench: `metrics/_benchmarks/bench_fairness_std_bessel.py`. Tests: `tests/metrics/test_biz_val_metrics_fairness_dispersion_std.py`.

- **`fast_classification_report` / `format_classification_report` (`mlframe.metrics`) now macro-average precision/recall/F1 over the classes PRESENT in `y_true` OR `y_pred` (`macro_over_present=True`); pass `macro_over_present=False` for the legacy divide-by-`nclasses` behaviour.** The macro averages divided the per-class P/R/F1 sum by `nclasses`, so a class declared in `nclasses` but absent from BOTH arrays (a common split artefact: a rare class missing from a CV fold / holdout, or a wider declared label set than the split realises) contributed a zeroed P/R/F1 and DEFLATED the macro means. This was the same defect already fixed for `balanced_accuracy` in the same kernel (which excludes `support==0` classes) -- left uncorrected on the macro P/R/F1 split. sklearn's `classification_report` macro-averages over the union of present classes; the new default reproduces it EXACTLY. Measured on `metrics/_benchmarks/bench_macro_avg_present_classes.py` (declared-but-absent scenarios {one/two/half classes absent} x nclasses {6,10} x 8 seeds, honest metric = |mlframe_macro - sklearn_macro| averaged over P/R/F1): the new policy matches sklearn in 48/48 cells (|err|=0.0000) vs the legacy mean |err| of 0.07 (one absent) to 0.38 (half absent) -- the deflation scales with the absent-class fraction. When every declared class is present the present-mask is all-True so both policies coincide (binary reports unaffected). Files: `metrics/_core_precision_mape.py` (`fast_classification_report` `macro_over_present` kwarg + default flip). Bench: `metrics/_benchmarks/bench_macro_avg_present_classes.py`. Tests: `tests/metrics/classification/test_biz_val_classification_macro_present.py`.

- **`kl_divergence` / `js_divergence` (`mlframe.metrics`, drift metrics) now default to a Miller-Madow bias-corrected estimate on the binned path (`bias_correction=True`); pass `bias_correction=False` for the legacy plug-in.** The binned plug-in KL(P||Q) and JS(P,Q) are POSITIVELY biased in finite samples -- the empirical histograms over-resolve the two distributions, so both sit clearly above the true divergence and stay strongly positive even when P and Q are drawn from the SAME distribution (true divergence 0, the drift "no-change" floor): at n=200 nbins=50 the plug-in self-divergence is ~0.225 (KL) / ~0.064 (JS) instead of 0, scaling up with the bin count and down with n. The leading 1/n bias is a Miller-Madow MI/entropy floor: JS equals the mutual information I(label; bin) so its bias is `(K-1)/(2N)` (K occupied pooled bins, N = np+nq); KL's is the sum of two entropy-bias contributions `(Kp-1)/(2 np) + (Kq-1)/(2 nq)` (the empirical -H(P) plus the cross term -H(P, Q_hat)). The default subtracts that floor and clamps at 0. Measured on `metrics/_benchmarks/bench_kl_js_divergence_debiased.py` (same-distribution + 0.5/1.0-sigma shift scenarios x 8 seeds x (n,nbins) in {200,1000}x{20,50}, honest metric = |estimate - Monte-Carlo ground-truth divergence|): the correction is closer to truth in 77/96 cells for BOTH KL and JS (80%), winning 8/8 at every same-distribution cell and every small-n/many-bins shifted cell, and cutting the same-distribution |bias| by ~7-11x (KL 0.225 -> 0.021, JS 0.064 -> 0.008 @ n=200 nbins=50) while keeping a genuine shift clearly flagged. The only losing cells are n=1000 nbins=20 shifted -- the low-bias regime where both estimators already sit near truth and the correction is a small over-shoot. The correction needs raw counts + sample sizes, so it is a no-op on the `pre_binned=True` path (normalised-probability inputs). Files: `metrics/_drift.py` (`kl_divergence` / `js_divergence` `bias_correction` kwarg + default flip, `_kl_mm_bias`). Bench: `metrics/_benchmarks/bench_kl_js_divergence_debiased.py`. Tests: `tests/metrics/test_biz_val_metrics_drift_divergence_debiased.py`.

- **`bootstrap_metric` / `bootstrap_metrics` (`mlframe.evaluation.bootstrap`) now default to the BCa (bias-corrected + accelerated) confidence interval (`method="bca"`); pass `method="percentile"` for the legacy Efron percentile interval.** The percentile interval ignores the skew + median-bias of the bootstrap sampling distribution, so on skewed / bounded metrics -- ROC-AUC near 1.0 (left-skewed, capped at 1.0) and Pearson correlation r -- it silently UNDER-COVERS the nominal level. BCa (Efron 1987) shifts the percentile cut-points using a bias-correction `z0 = Phi^-1(#{resample<point}/B)` and an acceleration `a` from the leave-one-out jackknife skew, recovering close-to-nominal coverage; it falls back to percentile automatically when its inputs are degenerate (no jackknife, constant resamples, non-finite z0/a). Measured empirical coverage (`evaluation/_benchmarks/bench_bootstrap_ci_coverage.py`, Monte-Carlo coverage of a nominal-95% CI, 600 trials x 5 seeds, honest metric = |coverage - 0.95|): in the skewed regime BCa is closer to nominal in 10/10 (scenario x seed) cells -- on AUC~0.97 @ n=150 percentile mean coverage 0.919 (gap 0.031) vs BCa 0.948 (gap 0.002), and on corr rho=0.90 @ n=40 percentile 0.930 vs BCa 0.938. The honest-diagnostics CI block (`training/honest_diagnostics.py`) inherits the better interval with no caller change; the only cost is an O(min(n, 2000)) jackknife pass per metric. Files: `evaluation/bootstrap.py` (`_ci_from_samples`, `_jackknife_metric`, `_jackknife_metric_idx`, `method` kwarg + default flip on both functions). Bench: `evaluation/_benchmarks/bench_bootstrap_ci_coverage.py`. Tests: `tests/evaluation/test_bootstrap.py` (BCa default + degenerate fallback + bootstrap_metrics parity), `tests/evaluation/test_biz_val_evaluation_bootstrap.py` (BCa coverage gap beats percentile on skewed AUC).

- **`entropy_calibration_index` (ECI) now defaults to a Miller-Madow bias-corrected entropy (`miller_madow=True`); set `miller_madow=False` for the legacy plug-in estimate.** ECI = log(bins) - H(binned PIT). A perfectly-calibrated model has a uniform PIT distribution, true entropy log(bins), and true ECI exactly 0. The plug-in Shannon entropy estimator H_hat = -sum p_i*log(p_i) is NEGATIVELY biased at finite n (leading term ~ -(K-1)/(2N)), so the plug-in ECI is POSITIVELY biased above 0 -- it spuriously reports miscalibration on a model that is in fact calibrated. The Miller-Madow correction H_mm = H_hat + (K_obs-1)/(2N) (K_obs = count of non-empty bins) cancels that leading 1/N bias term, pulling ECI back toward the true 0. Measured on `_benchmarks/bench_eci_miller_madow.py` (uniform PIT, true ECI 0, n=500 x 7 seeds x bins {10,20}, honest metric = |ECI - 0|): MM is closer to the true 0 in 12/14 cells (majority at BOTH bin counts) and cuts the mean |ECI| from 0.0132 (plug-in) to 0.0039 (MM), ~3.4x. Files: `calibration/quality.py`. Bench: `calibration/_benchmarks/bench_eci_miller_madow.py`. Tests: `tests/calibration/test_biz_val_calibration_eci_miller_madow.py`.

- **Headline Brier reliability/resolution in `fast_calibration_report` now default to a bias-corrected decomposition (`compute_brier_decomposition_debiased`, `brier_debiased=True`); set `brier_debiased=False` to recover the legacy plug-in REL/RES.** The plug-in binned Murphy decomposition `REL=sum_b w_b (conf_b-acc_b)^2` and `RES=sum_b w_b (acc_b-base_rate)^2` carry the SAME positive per-bin Bernoulli sampling-noise inflation that overstates the plug-in ECE -- within each bin `acc_b` is a noisy rate, so `E[(conf_b-acc_b)^2]=(conf_b-true_acc_b)^2+Var(acc_b)` and a perfectly calibrated model (true REL 0) reports a spurious positive REL that grows with `nbins`. The new kernel subtracts the unbiased within-bin variance `Var(acc_b)=acc_b*(1-acc_b)/(n_b-1)` from BOTH REL and RES (Broecker 2009 "Reliability, sufficiency, and the decomposition of proper scores", QJRMS), so `REL-RES`, `UNC` and `BinnedBrier` are all preserved EXACTLY (only the split between the now-unbiased reliability and resolution shifts) and the Murphy identity `REL-RES+UNC==BinnedBrier` still holds; REL is clamped at 0 (true reliability is non-negative). It uses the SAME data-adaptive equal-width grid as `compute_ece_and_brier_decomposition`. Measured on `_benchmarks/bench_brier_decomp_debiased.py` (3 perfectly-calibrated + 2 miscalibrated synthetics x 8 seeds x nbins {10,15,20}, honest metric = |estimated REL - fine-grid-ground-truth REL|, true REL 0 on the calibrated scenarios): debiased wins the MAJORITY of cells at every bin count (34/40 @ nbins=10, 35/40 @ 15, 36/40 @ 20) and more than HALVES the REL bias on every scenario (e.g. @ nbins=15: uniform-calibrated plug-in mean bias 0.00123 -> debiased 0.00057, beta-rare 0.00128 -> 0.00093, bimodal 0.00113 -> 0.00060, miscal-sigmoid 0.00258 -> 0.00144, miscal-overconf 0.00203 -> 0.00046), while staying large on grossly miscalibrated models (the true-gap term dominates the small correction). Re-exported from `mlframe.metrics.core` / `.metrics.calibration`. Files: `metrics/calibration/_calibration_metrics.py` (`compute_brier_decomposition_debiased`), `metrics/classification/_classification_report.py` (`brier_debiased` toggle + default flip), `metrics/calibration/__init__.py` + `metrics/core.py` (re-export), `metrics/_core_numba_warmup.py` (prewarm). Bench: `metrics/_benchmarks/bench_brier_decomp_debiased.py`. Tests: `tests/metrics/test_biz_val_calibration_brier_decomp_debiased.py`.

- **Headline ECE in `fast_calibration_report` now defaults to a bias-corrected estimator (`compute_ece_debiased`, `ece_debiased=True`); set `ece_debiased=False` to recover the legacy plug-in value.** The plug-in binned ECE `sum_b (n_b/N)*|conf_b - acc_b|` is a POSITIVELY biased estimator of the population ECE: `acc_b` is a noisy Bernoulli rate and the absolute value of a noisy gap is inflated by the per-bin sampling noise (Jensen), so a perfectly calibrated model (true ECE 0) reports a spurious positive ECE that grows with `nbins`. The new kernel subtracts the per-bin variance floor `Var(acc_b)=acc_b*(1-acc_b)/(n_b-1)` from the squared gap and clamps at 0 (Kumar et al. NeurIPS 2019 "Verified Uncertainty Calibration"; Roelofs et al. AISTATS 2022), using the SAME data-adaptive equal-width grid as `compute_ece_and_brier_decomposition` so the two are directly comparable. Measured on `_benchmarks/bench_ece_debiased.py` (3 perfectly-calibrated + 2 miscalibrated synthetics x 7-8 seeds x nbins {10,15,20}, honest metric = |estimate - fine-grid-ground-truth ECE|): debiased wins the MAJORITY of cells at every bin count (29/35 @ nbins=10, 28/35 @ 15, 32/40 @ 20) and more than HALVES the bias on the calibrated scenarios (e.g. uniform-calibrated @ nbins=15: plug-in mean bias 0.0294 -> debiased 0.0127; beta-rare 0.0128 -> 0.0055; bimodal 0.0196 -> 0.0082), while staying large on a grossly miscalibrated model (the true-gap term dominates the small correction). The Brier decomposition REL/RES/UNC stays on the plug-in grid so the Murphy identity `REL-RES+UNC==BinnedBrier` still holds exactly. Re-exported from `mlframe.metrics.core` / `.metrics.calibration`. Files: `metrics/calibration/_calibration_metrics.py` (`compute_ece_debiased`), `metrics/classification/_classification_report.py` (`ece_debiased` toggle + default flip), `metrics/calibration/__init__.py` + `metrics/core.py` (re-export), `metrics/_core_numba_warmup.py` (prewarm). Bench: `metrics/_benchmarks/bench_ece_debiased.py`. Tests: `tests/metrics/test_biz_val_calibration_ece_debiased.py`.

- **`LeakageSafeEncoder.transform` (held-out scoring path) replaces its per-row Python dict-lookup loop with a `pd.factorize` + per-unique-category gather (~2.4x on `woe`, ~1.3x on `target_mean`/`target_james_stein` at n=10M; bit-identical).** `_encode_with_full_train_stat` ran `for i, c in enumerate(cats): out[i] = ...dict.get(c)...` over EVERY held-out row -- a 10M-iteration pure-Python loop at production scoring time (~20s `target_mean`, ~40s `woe` at n=10M). The per-row encoding is a deterministic function of the category string alone, so the value is now computed once over the (small) unique-category set and gathered back via `pd.factorize(cats)` + numpy take. Bit-identical BY CONSTRUCTION (same per-category arithmetic, same unseen-category prior/log-odds fallback, same Laplace clip) -- verified `max|OLD-NEW| = 0.0` (exact `==`) across `target_mean` / `woe` / `target_james_stein` / `target_m_estimate`, cardinalities {1, 5, 200}, unseen categories and ties. End-to-end `transform()` at n=10M: `woe` 40.2s -> 17.0s (2.36x), `target_mean` 20.2s -> 15.5s (1.31x); the kernel-only loop replacement is the larger win, the residual `transform` time is the `_categorical_to_string_array` per-element pass (unchanged). The legacy per-row path is retained as `_encode_per_row` (pandas-unavailable fallback). Files: `training/feature_handling/target_encoders.py` (`_encode_with_full_train_stat` dispatch + `_encode_vectorised` + `_encode_per_row`). Bench: `training/feature_handling/_benchmarks/bench_encode_full_train_stat_transform.py`. Tests: `tests/training/test_target_encoder_vectorised_transform.py` (bit-identity vectorised-vs-per-row across methods/cardinalities + a spy sensor that the per-row loop never runs over the full array).

- **`CompositeTargetDiscovery` knn-path per-base `mi_y` baseline is now computed ONCE instead of re-swept per base candidate (~5.6x on that sub-phase; bit-identical, same specs).** On the knn screening path (`mi_estimator='knn'`) the per-base baseline `MI(y, X_remaining)` was recomputed for EVERY base candidate, each call invoking sklearn's Kraskov `mutual_info_regression` once per remaining feature column (~0.45s/column at the 100k MI-screen subsample). Since per-column `MI(y, x_j)` is base-INVARIANT, that was `n_bases` redundant full per-column sweeps. The bin path already avoided this via the precomputed per-feature vector + exclude-aggregate decomposition; the new `_mi_per_feature_knn` extends the identical trick to the knn path: compute the per-column vector once, then derive each base's baseline by aggregating over its surviving (base-dropped, dedup-kept) original-column indices. Bit-identical by construction (each column's MI is independent of the others; same sklearn calls, same per-pair finite masking, same mean/sum reduction order) -- verified `max|OLD-NEW| = 0.0` over 6 bases and identical selected specs + mi_gain values end-to-end. Isolated A/B (`discovery/_benchmarks/bench_iter96_knn_mi_y_baseline_recompute.py`): 29.4s -> 5.2s = 5.64x at n=20k / 53 cols / 6 bases. The default `mi_estimator='bin'` path is untouched (the new branch is gated on `not _bin_estimator`). Files: `discovery/screening.py` (`_mi_per_feature_knn`), `discovery/_fit.py` (compute-once + per-base index-exclusion aggregate), `discovery/__init__.py` (re-export). Tests: `tests/training/composite/test_knn_mi_y_baseline_compute_once.py` (bit-identity vs per-base recompute across mean/sum x drop indices; per-column vs single-call match; compute-once spy sensor -- pre-fix red).

- **MRMR discrete-structural FE suite gets a single master switch `fe_discrete_structural_operators_enable` (default True).** The four discrete operators (pairwise-modular, integer-lattice, row-argmax, conditional-gate) added ~15 ctor flags that were discoverable only by reading source. The master switch toggles ALL FOUR at once: False disables every discrete operator regardless of its individual `fe_*_enable` flag (pure classical FE in one knob), True (default) lets the per-operator flags govern individually. Default-True ANDed with the all-default-True individual flags is byte-identical to prior behaviour (purely additive). A consolidated docstring at the switch summarises what the four operators capture (modular periods/parity, gcd/lcm grid alignment, ordinal row-comparisons, data-dependent regime switches), that all are cheap-first / leak-free / budget-guarded / classification+regression, and the measured downstream model lift. Files: `mrmr/_mrmr_class.py` (ctor flag + doc), `_mrmr_fit_impl/_fit_impl_core.py` (the four FE blocks gated on the master). Tests: `tests/feature_selection/test_biz_val_discrete_fe_master_switch.py` (master ON emits, OFF suppresses all four, OFF overrides an explicit individual enable).

- **Wrapper-selector target-type robustness: a supported target or realistic degenerate frame no longer crashes a public wrapper selector with an obscure internal traceback; unsupported target SHAPES now raise a clear, actionable error at fit entry.** A target-type / edge-case audit (the same discipline that hardened the MRMR FE operators) ran the 6 public wrapper selectors (RFECV, BorutaShap, ShapProxiedFS, HybridSelector, StabilityMRMR, GroupAwareMRMR) against a (target-type x degenerate-frame) matrix. StabilityMRMR, GroupAwareMRMR, BorutaShap and RFECV were already robust (single-class raises a clear MRMR error; multilabel / regression / NaN / p>n / collinear / dup / all-NaN all complete or error clearly). Two selectors had obscure-crash gaps, both fixed: (1) **ShapProxiedFS** silently let a 2D (multilabel / multi-output) y survive `np.unique`/`astype` and then blew up deep inside a numba proxy-loss kernel with an opaque `TypingError`; `_coerce_target` now rejects `y.ndim>1` up front with a clear single-output ValueError (with a `y.ravel()` hint for a single-column 2D target). (2) **HybridSelector** is intrinsically classification-only (stratified split + `LGBMClassifier` + binary ShapProxiedFS / BorutaShap members), but a continuous target crashed inside `train_test_split`'s stratify ("least populated class has 1 member") and a 2D y crashed the LGBM 1d-array check -- both opaque; `fit` now detects the unsupported target via `type_of_target` and raises a clear "classification targets only" / "single-output classification" ValueError, AND its shared permutation-FI split falls back to an unstratified split when any class has <2 members (rare-class / count-like multiclass target now completes instead of crashing). Files: `shap_proxied_fs/__init__.py` (`_coerce_target`), `hybrid_selector.py` (`fit` task guard + `_shared_perm_fi` stratify fallback). Tests: `tests/feature_selection/test_wrapper_target_type_guards.py` (5 regression sensors).

- **The four default-ON MRMR class-MI FE operators (pairwise-modular, integer-lattice, row-argmax, conditional-gate) now WORK on CONTINUOUS / REGRESSION targets via quantile-binned y, instead of skipping them.** They gate candidates on plug-in class-MI (`_mi_classif_batch`), which needs a discrete y. The prior fix made them SKIP continuous y (the int64 cast collapsed a continuous target to ~n bogus classes -> inflated-garbage MI / segfault / a tau-sweep hang), which left REGRESSION / QUANTILE / COUNT / ORDINAL users with NONE of these edges even though mod / gcd / argmax / regime structure exists in continuous targets too. The fix quantile-bins a continuous 1D y ONCE per fit (`bin_y_for_class_mi`, `pd.qcut` at `quantization_nbins`, mirroring the MRMR core's own continuous-y relevance binning) into a proper discrete target, then scores against the SAME `_mi_classif_batch` machinery; the binned labels are reused across the whole candidate scan. A 1D CLASSIFICATION y still feeds the kernel directly -- BIT-IDENTICAL to before (the binary / multiclass selection path does not move). A 2D y (multilabel / multi-target regression) STAYS skipped (binning a label matrix is out of scope). The detectors' permutation-null + best-existing-op + margin gates all run on the same binned y, so specificity is preserved on regression. Measured (`_benchmarks/bench_fe_operators_regression.py`, 600 rows, nbins=10): on the matching regression synthetic each operator DETECTS with strong MI lift over the best-existing-op (pairwise-modular `y=3*(a mod 7)+noise` 5.94x, integer-lattice `y=gcd(a,b)+noise` 7.64x, row-argmax `y=5*argmax+noise` 1.77x, conditional-gate `y=a if c>median else b` 2.77x) and emits 0 spurious features on smooth/noise continuous controls across 3 seeds; all fits < 0.1s. Companion fix to the `_responded` gate (below) was required to hit 0 FP on regression. Threaded `quantization_nbins` from the MRMR instance into all four operator orchestrators. Files: `_fe_accuracy_gate.py` (new `bin_y_for_class_mi`, `class_mi_fe_applicable` now admits continuous 1D y), `_mrmr_fit_impl/_fit_impl_core.py` (4 blocks bin y before the call). Tests: the target-type robustness classes in the 3 operator test files now assert regression DETECTS (matching synthetic) + smooth control STAYS SPECIFIC + 2D y SKIPPED + no hang/crash on every type.
